<a id="user-guide-sql-operators"></a>

<!-- source_url: https://datafusion.apache.org/user-guide/sql/operators.html -->

<!-- page_index: 25 -->

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--operators-and-literals"></a>

# Operators and Literals

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--numerical-operators"></a>

## Numerical Operators

- [+ (plus)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-plus)
- [- (minus)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-minus)
- [\* (multiply)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-multiply)
- [/ (divide)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-divide)
- [% (modulo)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-modulo)

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section"></a>

### `+`

Addition

```
> SELECT 1 + 2; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (1) + Int64 (2) | + ---------------------+ | 3 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-2"></a>

### `-`

Subtraction

```
> SELECT 4 -3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (4) -Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 1 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-3"></a>

### `*`

Multiplication

```
> SELECT 2 * 3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (2) * Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 6 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-4"></a>

### `/`

Division (integer division truncates toward zero)

```
> SELECT 8/4; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (8)/Int64 (4) | + ---------------------+ | 2 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-5"></a>

### `%`

Modulo (remainder)

```
> SELECT 7 % 3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (7) % Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 1 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--comparison-operators"></a>

## Comparison Operators

- [= (equal)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-eq)
- [!= (not equal)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-neq)
- [< (less than)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-lt)
- [<= (less than or equal to)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-le)
- [> (greater than)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-gt)
- [>= (greater than or equal to)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-ge)
- [<=> (three-way comparison, alias for IS NOT DISTINCT FROM)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-spaceship)
- [IS DISTINCT FROM](#user-guide-sql-operators--is-distinct-from)
- [IS NOT DISTINCT FROM](#user-guide-sql-operators--is-not-distinct-from)
- [~ (regex match)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-re-match)
- [~\* (regex case-insensitive match)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-re-match-i)
- [!~ (not regex match)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-re-not-match)
- [!~\* (not regex case-insensitive match)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-re-not-match-i)

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-6"></a>

### `=`

Equal

```
> SELECT 1=1; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (1)=Int64 (1) | + ---------------------+ | true | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-7"></a>

### `!=`

Not Equal

```
> SELECT 1 !=2; + ----------------------+ | Int64 (1) !=Int64 (2) | + ----------------------+ | true | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-8"></a>

### `<`

Less Than

```
> SELECT 3 < 4; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (3) < Int64 (4) | + ---------------------+ | true | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-9"></a>

### `<=`

Less Than or Equal To

```
> SELECT 3 <=3; + ----------------------+ | Int64 (3) <=Int64 (3) | + ----------------------+ | true | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-10"></a>

### `>`

Greater Than

```
> SELECT 6 > 5; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (6) > Int64 (5) | + ---------------------+ | true | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-11"></a>

### `>=`

Greater Than or Equal To

```
> SELECT 5 >=5; + ----------------------+ | Int64 (5) >=Int64 (5) | + ----------------------+ | true | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-12"></a>

### `<=>`

Three-way comparison operator. A NULL-safe operator that returns true if both operands are equal or both are NULL, false otherwise.

```
> SELECT NULL <=> NULL; + --------------------------------+ | NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL | + --------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------+
```

```
> SELECT 1 <=> NULL; + ------------------------------------+ | Int64 (1) IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL | + ------------------------------------+ | false | + ------------------------------------+
```

```
> SELECT 1 <=> 2; + ----------------------------------------+ | Int64 (1) IS NOT DISTINCT FROM Int64 (2) | + ----------------------------------------+ | false | + ----------------------------------------+
```

```
> SELECT 1 <=> 1; + ----------------------------------------+ | Int64 (1) IS NOT DISTINCT FROM Int64 (1) | + ----------------------------------------+ | true | + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--is-distinct-from"></a>

### `IS DISTINCT FROM`

Guarantees the result of a comparison is `true` or `false` and not an empty set

```
> SELECT 0 IS DISTINCT FROM NULL; + --------------------------------+ | Int64 (0) IS DISTINCT FROM NULL | + --------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--is-not-distinct-from"></a>

### `IS NOT DISTINCT FROM`

The negation of `IS DISTINCT FROM`

```
> SELECT NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL; + --------------------------------+ | NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL | + --------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-13"></a>

### `~`

Regex Match

```
> SELECT 'datafusion' ~ '^datafusion(-cli)*'; + -------------------------------------------------+ | Utf8 ("datafusion") ~ Utf8 ("^datafusion(-cli)*") | + -------------------------------------------------+ | true | + -------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-14"></a>

### `~*`

Regex Case-Insensitive Match

```
> SELECT 'datafusion' ~* '^DATAFUSION(-cli)*'; + --------------------------------------------------+ | Utf8 ("datafusion") ~* Utf8 ("^DATAFUSION(-cli)*") | + --------------------------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-15"></a>

### `!~`

Not Regex Match

```
> SELECT 'datafusion' !~ '^DATAFUSION(-cli)*'; + --------------------------------------------------+ | Utf8 ("datafusion") !~ Utf8 ("^DATAFUSION(-cli)*") | + --------------------------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-16"></a>

### `!~*`

Not Regex Case-Insensitive Match

```
> SELECT 'datafusion' !~* '^DATAFUSION(-cli)+'; + ---------------------------------------------------+ | Utf8 ("datafusion") !~* Utf8 ("^DATAFUSION(-cli)+") | + ---------------------------------------------------+ | true | + ---------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-17"></a>

### `~~`

Like Match

```
SELECT
'datafusion'
~~
'dat_f%n';
+ ---------------------------------------+ |
Utf8 ("datafusion")
~~
Utf8 ("dat_f%n")
| + ---------------------------------------+ |
true
| + ---------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-18"></a>

### `~~*`

Case-Insensitive Like Match

```
SELECT
'datafusion'
~~*
'Dat_F%n';
+ ----------------------------------------+ |
Utf8 ("datafusion")
~~*
Utf8 ("Dat_F%n")
| + ----------------------------------------+ |
true
| + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-19"></a>

### `!~~`

Not Like Match

```
SELECT
'datafusion'
!~~
'Dat_F%n';
+ ----------------------------------------+ |
Utf8 ("datafusion")
!~~
Utf8 ("Dat_F%n")
| + ----------------------------------------+ |
true
| + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-20"></a>

### `!~~*`

Not Case-Insensitive Like Match

```
SELECT
'datafusion'
!~~*
'Dat%F_n';
+ -----------------------------------------+ |
Utf8 ("datafusion")
!~~*
Utf8 ("Dat%F_n")
| + -----------------------------------------+ |
true
| + -----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--logical-operators"></a>

## Logical Operators

- [AND](#user-guide-sql-operators--and)
- [OR](#user-guide-sql-operators--or)

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--and"></a>

### `AND`

Logical And

```
> SELECT true AND true; + ---------------------------------+ | Boolean (true) AND Boolean (true) | + ---------------------------------+ | true | + ---------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--or"></a>

### `OR`

Logical Or

```
> SELECT false OR true; + ---------------------------------+ | Boolean (false) OR Boolean (true) | + ---------------------------------+ | true | + ---------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--bitwise-operators"></a>

## Bitwise Operators

- [& (bitwise and)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-bit-and)
- [| (bitwise or)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-bit-or)
- [# (bitwise xor)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-bit-xor)
- [>> (bitwise shift right)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-shift-r)
- [<< (bitwise shift left)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-shift-l)

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-21"></a>

### `&`

Bitwise And

```
> SELECT 5 & 3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (5) & Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 1 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-22"></a>

### `|`

Bitwise Or

```
> SELECT 5 | 3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (5) | Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 7 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-23"></a>

### `#`

Bitwise Xor (interchangeable with `^`)

```
> SELECT 5 # 3; + ---------------------+ | Int64 (5) # Int64 (3) | + ---------------------+ | 6 | + ---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-24"></a>

### `>>`

Bitwise Shift Right

```
> SELECT 5 >> 3; + ----------------------+ | Int64 (5) >> Int64 (3) | + ----------------------+ | 0 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-25"></a>

### `<<`

Bitwise Shift Left

```
> SELECT 5 << 3; + ----------------------+ | Int64 (5) << Int64 (3) | + ----------------------+ | 40 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--other-operators"></a>

## Other Operators

- [|| (string concatenation)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-str-cat)
- [@> (array contains)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-arr-contains)
- [<@ (array is contained by)](#user-guide-sql-operators--op-arr-contained-by)

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-26"></a>

### `||`

String Concatenation

```
> SELECT 'Hello, ' || 'DataFusion!'; + ----------------------------------------+ | Utf8 ("Hello, ") || Utf8 ("DataFusion!") | + ----------------------------------------+ | Hello,DataFusion ! | + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-27"></a>

### `@>`

Array Contains

```
> SELECT make_array (1,2,3) @> make_array (1,3 ); + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | make_array (Int64 (1 ),Int64 (2 ),Int64 (3 )) @> make_array (Int64 (1 ),Int64 (3 )) | + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | true | + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--section-28"></a>

### `<@`

Array Is Contained By

```
> SELECT make_array (1,3) <@ make_array (1,2,3 ); + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | make_array (Int64 (1 ),Int64 (3 )) <@ make_array (Int64 (1 ),Int64 (2 ),Int64 (3 )) | + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | true | + -------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--literals"></a>

## Literals

Use single quotes for literal values. For example, the string `foo bar` is
referred to using `'foo bar'`

```

select
 
'foo'
;
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-operators--escaping"></a>

### Escaping

Unlike many other languages, SQL literals do not by default support C-style escape
sequences such as `\n` for newline. Instead all characters in a `'` string are treated
literally.

To escape `'` in SQL literals, use `''`:

```
> select 'it''s escaped'; + ----------------------+ | Utf8 ("it's escaped") | + ----------------------+ | it ' s escaped | + ----------------------+ 1 row (s) fetched.
```

Strings such as `foo\nbar` mean `\` followed by `n` (not newline):

```
> select 'foo\nbar'; + ------------------+ | Utf8 ("foo\nbar") | + ------------------+ | foo \ nbar | + ------------------+ 1 row (s) fetched.Elapsed 0.005 seconds.
```

To add escaped characters such as newline or tab, instead of `\n` you use the
`E` style strings. For example, to add the text with a newline

```
foo
bar
```

You can use `E'foo\nbar'`

```
> select E 'foo\nbar'; + -----------------+ | Utf8 ("foo bar") | + -----------------+ | foo bar | + -----------------+
```

---

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions"></a>

<!-- source_url: https://datafusion.apache.org/user-guide/sql/aggregate_functions.html -->

<!-- page_index: 26 -->

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aggregate-functions"></a>

# Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions operate on a set of values to compute a single result.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--filter-clause"></a>

## Filter clause

Aggregate functions support the SQL `FILTER (WHERE ...)` clause to restrict which input rows contribute to the aggregate result.

```
function ([exprs ])
FILTER
(WHERE
condition)
```

Example:

```
SELECT
sum (salary)
FILTER
(WHERE
salary
> 0) AS sum_positive_salaries,count (*) FILTER (WHERE active) AS active_count FROM employees;
```

Note: When no rows pass the filter, `COUNT` returns `0` while `SUM`/`AVG`/`MIN`/`MAX` return `NULL`.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--within-group-ordered-set-aggregates"></a>

## WITHIN GROUP / Ordered-set aggregates

Some aggregate functions accept the SQL `WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...)` clause to specify the ordering the
aggregate relies on. In DataFusion this is opt-in: only aggregate functions whose implementation returns
`true` from `AggregateUDFImpl::supports_within_group_clause()` accept the `WITHIN GROUP` clause. Attempting to
use `WITHIN GROUP` with a regular aggregate (for example, `SELECT SUM(x) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY x)`) will fail
during planning with an error: “WITHIN GROUP is only supported for ordered-set aggregate functions”.

Currently, the built-in aggregate functions that support `WITHIN GROUP` are:

- `percentile_cont` — exact percentile aggregate (also available as `percentile_cont(column, percentile)`)
- `approx_percentile_cont` — approximate percentile using the t-digest algorithm
- `approx_percentile_cont_with_weight` — approximate weighted percentile using the t-digest algorithm

Note: rank-like functions such as `rank()`, `dense_rank()`, and `percent_rank()` are window functions and
use the `OVER (...)` clause; they are not ordered-set aggregates that accept `WITHIN GROUP` in DataFusion.

Example (ordered-set aggregate):

```
percentile_cont (0.5)
WITHIN
GROUP
(ORDER
BY
value)
```

Example (invalid usage — planner will error):

```
-- This will fail: SUM is not an ordered-set aggregate SELECT
SUM (x)
WITHIN
GROUP
(ORDER
BY
x)
FROM
t;
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--array_agg"></a>

### `array_agg`

Returns an array created from the expression elements. If ordering is required, elements are inserted in the specified order.
This aggregation function can only mix DISTINCT and ORDER BY if the ordering expression is exactly the same as the argument expression.

```
array_agg (expression
[ORDER
BY
expression ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT array_agg (column_name ORDER BY other_column) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------------------+ | array_agg (column_name ORDER BY other_column) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | [element1,element2,element3] | + -----------------------------------------------+> SELECT array_agg (DISTINCT column_name ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + --------------------------------------------------------+ | array_agg (DISTINCT column_name ORDER BY column_name) | + --------------------------------------------------------+ | [element1,element2,element3] | + --------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--avg"></a>

### `avg`

Returns the average of numeric values in the specified column.

```

avg
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-2"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-2"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT avg (column_name) FROM table_name; + ---------------------------+ | avg (column_name) | + ---------------------------+ | 42.75 | + ---------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases"></a>

#### Aliases

- mean

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--bit_and"></a>

### `bit_and`

Computes the bitwise AND of all non-null input values.

```

bit_and
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-3"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Integer expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--bit_or"></a>

### `bit_or`

Computes the bitwise OR of all non-null input values.

```

bit_or
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-4"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Integer expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--bit_xor"></a>

### `bit_xor`

Computes the bitwise exclusive OR of all non-null input values.

```

bit_xor
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-5"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Integer expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--bool_and"></a>

### `bool_and`

Returns true if all non-null input values are true, otherwise false.

```

bool_and
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-6"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-3"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT bool_and (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------------+ | bool_and (column_name) | + ----------------------------+ | true | + ----------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--bool_or"></a>

### `bool_or`

Returns true if all non-null input values are true, otherwise false.

```

bool_and
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-7"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-4"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT bool_and (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------------+ | bool_and (column_name) | + ----------------------------+ | true | + ----------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--count"></a>

### `count`

Returns the number of non-null values in the specified column. To include null values in the total count, use `count(*)`.

```

count
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-8"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-5"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT count (column_name) FROM table_name; + -----------------------+ | count (column_name) | + -----------------------+ | 100 | + -----------------------+> SELECT count (*) FROM table_name; + ------------------+ | count (*) | + ------------------+ | 120 | + ------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--first_value"></a>

### `first_value`

Returns the first element in an aggregation group according to the requested ordering. If no ordering is given, returns an arbitrary element from the group.

```
first_value (expression
[ORDER
BY
expression ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-9"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-6"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT first_value (column_name ORDER BY other_column) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------------------+ | first_value (column_name ORDER BY other_column) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | first_element | + -----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--grouping"></a>

### `grouping`

Returns 1 if the data is aggregated across the specified column, or 0 if it is not aggregated in the result set.

```

grouping
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-10"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression to evaluate whether data is aggregated across the specified column. Can be a constant, column, or function.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-7"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT column_name,GROUPING (column_name) AS group_column FROM table_name GROUP BY GROUPING SETS ((column_name ),()); + -------------+-------------+ | column_name | group_column | + -------------+-------------+ | value1 | 0 | | value2 | 0 | | NULL | 1 | + -------------+-------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--last_value"></a>

### `last_value`

Returns the last element in an aggregation group according to the requested ordering. If no ordering is given, returns an arbitrary element from the group.

```
last_value (expression
[ORDER
BY
expression ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-11"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-8"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT last_value (column_name ORDER BY other_column) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------------------+ | last_value (column_name ORDER BY other_column) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | last_element | + -----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--max"></a>

### `max`

Returns the maximum value in the specified column.

```

max
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-12"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-9"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT max (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------+ | max (column_name) | + ----------------------+ | 150 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--mean"></a>

### `mean`

*Alias of [avg](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--avg).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--median"></a>

### `median`

Returns the median value in the specified column.

```

median
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-13"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-10"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT median (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------+ | median (column_name) | + ----------------------+ | 45.5 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--min"></a>

### `min`

Returns the minimum value in the specified column.

```

min
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-14"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-11"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT min (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------+ | min (column_name) | + ----------------------+ | 12 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--percentile_cont"></a>

### `percentile_cont`

Returns the exact percentile of input values, interpolating between values if needed.

```
percentile_cont (percentile)
WITHIN
GROUP
(ORDER
BY
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-15"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **percentile**: Percentile to compute. Must be a float value between 0 and 1 (inclusive).

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-12"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT percentile_cont (0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | percentile_cont (0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) | + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | 45.5 | + ----------------------------------------------------------+
```

An alternate syntax is also supported:

```
> SELECT percentile_cont (column_name,0.75) FROM table_name; + ---------------------------------------+ | percentile_cont (column_name,0.75) | + ---------------------------------------+ | 45.5 | + ---------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases-2"></a>

#### Aliases

- quantile\_cont

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--quantile_cont"></a>

### `quantile_cont`

*Alias of [percentile\_cont](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--percentile-cont).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--string_agg"></a>

### `string_agg`

Concatenates the values of string expressions and places separator values between them. If ordering is required, strings are concatenated in the specified order. This aggregation function can only mix DISTINCT and ORDER BY if the ordering expression is exactly the same as the first argument expression.

```
string_agg ([DISTINCT]
expression,
delimiter
[ORDER
BY
expression ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-16"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The string expression to concatenate. Can be a column or any valid string expression.
- **delimiter**: A literal string used as a separator between the concatenated values.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-13"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT string_agg (name,', ') AS names_list FROM employee; + --------------------------+ | names_list | + --------------------------+ | Alice,Bob,Bob,Charlie | + --------------------------+> SELECT string_agg (name,', ' ORDER BY name DESC) AS names_list FROM employee; + --------------------------+ | names_list | + --------------------------+ | Charlie,Bob,Bob,Alice | + --------------------------+> SELECT string_agg (DISTINCT name,', ' ORDER BY name DESC) AS names_list FROM employee; + --------------------------+ | names_list | + --------------------------+ | Charlie,Bob,Alice | + --------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--sum"></a>

### `sum`

Returns the sum of all values in the specified column.

```

sum
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-17"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-14"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT sum (column_name) FROM table_name; + -----------------------+ | sum (column_name) | + -----------------------+ | 12345 | + -----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var"></a>

### `var`

Returns the statistical sample variance of a set of numbers.

```

var
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-18"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases-3"></a>

#### Aliases

- var\_sample
- var\_samp

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var_pop"></a>

### `var_pop`

Returns the statistical population variance of a set of numbers.

```

var_pop
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-19"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases-4"></a>

#### Aliases

- var\_population

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var_population"></a>

### `var_population`

*Alias of [var\_pop](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var-pop).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var_samp"></a>

### `var_samp`

*Alias of [var](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var_sample"></a>

### `var_sample`

*Alias of [var](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--var).*

## Statistical Functions

- [corr](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--corr)

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--corr"></a>

### `corr`

Returns the coefficient of correlation between two numeric values.

```
corr (expression1,
expression2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-20"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: First expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Second expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-15"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT corr (column1,column2) FROM table_name; + --------------------------------+ | corr (column1,column2) | + --------------------------------+ | 0.85 | + --------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--covar"></a>

### `covar`

*Alias of [covar\_samp](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--covar-samp).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--covar_pop"></a>

### `covar_pop`

Returns the sample covariance of a set of number pairs.

```
covar_samp (expression1,
expression2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-21"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: First expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Second expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-16"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT covar_samp (column1,column2) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------+ | covar_samp (column1,column2) | + -----------------------------------+ | 8.25 | + -----------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--covar_samp"></a>

### `covar_samp`

Returns the sample covariance of a set of number pairs.

```
covar_samp (expression1,
expression2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-22"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: First expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Second expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-17"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT covar_samp (column1,column2) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------+ | covar_samp (column1,column2) | + -----------------------------------+ | 8.25 | + -----------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases-5"></a>

#### Aliases

- covar

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--nth_value"></a>

### `nth_value`

Returns the nth value in a group of values.

```
nth_value (expression,
n
ORDER
BY
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-23"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The column or expression to retrieve the nth value from.
- **n**: The position (nth) of the value to retrieve, based on the ordering.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-18"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT dept_id,salary,NTH_VALUE (salary,2) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary ASC) AS second_salary_by_dept FROM employee; + ---------+--------+-------------------------+ | dept_id | salary | second_salary_by_dept | + ---------+--------+-------------------------+ | 1 | 30000 | NULL | | 1 | 40000 | 40000 | | 1 | 50000 | 40000 | | 2 | 35000 | NULL | | 2 | 45000 | 45000 | + ---------+--------+-------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_avgx"></a>

### `regr_avgx`

Computes the average of the independent variable (input) expression\_x for the non-null paired data points.

```
regr_avgx (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-24"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-19"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
daily_sales (day
int,
total_sales
int)
as
values
(1,100 ),
(2,150 ),
(3,200 ),
(4,NULL ),
(5,250 ); select
*
from
daily_sales;
+ -----+-------------+ |
day
|
total_sales
| |
--- | ----------- | |
1
|
100
| |
2
|
150
| |
3
|
200
| |
4
|
NULL
| |
5
|
250
| + -----+-------------+ SELECT
regr_avgx (total_sales,
day)
AS
avg_day
FROM
daily_sales;
+ ----------+ |
avg_day
| + ----------+ |
2.75
| + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_avgy"></a>

### `regr_avgy`

Computes the average of the dependent variable (output) expression\_y for the non-null paired data points.

```
regr_avgy (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-25"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-20"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
daily_temperature (day
int,
temperature
int)
as
values
(1,30 ),
(2,32 ),
(3,
NULL ),
(4,35 ),
(5,36 ); select
*
from
daily_temperature;
+ -----+-------------+ |
day
|
temperature
| |
--- | ----------- | |
1
|
30
| |
2
|
32
| |
3
|
NULL
| |
4
|
35
| |
5
|
36
| + -----+-------------+ -- temperature as Dependent Variable(Y), day as Independent Variable(X) SELECT
regr_avgy (temperature,
day)
AS
avg_temperature
FROM
daily_temperature;
+ -----------------+ |
avg_temperature
| + -----------------+ |
33.25
| + -----------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_count"></a>

### `regr_count`

Counts the number of non-null paired data points.

```
regr_count (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-26"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-21"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
daily_metrics (day
int,
user_signups
int)
as
values
(1,100 ),
(2,120 ),
(3,
NULL ),
(4,110 ),
(5,NULL ); select
*
from
daily_metrics;
+ -----+---------------+ |
day
|
user_signups
| |
--- | ------------- | |
1
|
100
| |
2
|
120
| |
3
|
NULL
| |
4
|
110
| |
5
|
NULL
| + -----+---------------+ SELECT
regr_count (user_signups,
day)
AS
valid_pairs
FROM
daily_metrics;
+ -------------+ |
valid_pairs
| + -------------+ |
3
| + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_intercept"></a>

### `regr_intercept`

Computes the y-intercept of the linear regression line. For the equation (y = kx + b), this function returns b.

```
regr_intercept (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-27"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-22"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
weekly_performance (week
int,
productivity_score
int)
as
values
(1,60 ),
(2,65 ),
(3,
70 ),
(4,75 ),
(5,80 ); select
*
from
weekly_performance;
+ ------+---------------------+ |
week
|
productivity_score
| |
---- | ------------------- | |
1
|
60
| |
2
|
65
| |
3
|
70
| |
4
|
75
| |
5
|
80
| + ------+---------------------+ SELECT
regr_intercept (productivity_score,
week)
AS
intercept
FROM
weekly_performance;
+ ----------+ | intercept | | intercept
| + ----------+ |
55
| + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_r2"></a>

### `regr_r2`

Computes the square of the correlation coefficient between the independent and dependent variables.

```
regr_r2 (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-28"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-23"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
weekly_performance (day
int
,user_signups
int)
as
values
(1,60 ),
(2,65 ),
(3,
70 ),
(4,75 ),
(5,80 ); select
*
from
weekly_performance;
+ -----+--------------+ |
day
|
user_signups
| + -----+--------------+ |
1
|
60
| |
2
|
65
| |
3
|
70
| |
4
|
75
| |
5
|
80
| + -----+--------------+ SELECT
regr_r2 (user_signups,
day)
AS
r_squared
FROM
weekly_performance;
+ ---------+ | r_squared | + ---------+ |
1.0
| + ---------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_slope"></a>

### `regr_slope`

Returns the slope of the linear regression line for non-null pairs in aggregate columns. Given input column Y and X: regr\_slope(Y, X) returns the slope (k in Y = k\*X + b) using minimal RSS fitting.

```
regr_slope (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-29"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-24"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
weekly_performance (day
int,
user_signups
int)
as
values
(1,60 ),
(2,65 ),
(3,
70 ),
(4,75 ),
(5,80 ); select
*
from
weekly_performance;
+ -----+--------------+ |
day
|
user_signups
| + -----+--------------+ |
1
|
60
| |
2
|
65
| |
3
|
70
| |
4
|
75
| |
5
|
80
| + -----+--------------+ SELECT
regr_slope (user_signups,
day)
AS
slope
FROM
weekly_performance;
+ --------+ |
slope
| + --------+ |
5.0
| + --------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_sxx"></a>

### `regr_sxx`

Computes the sum of squares of the independent variable.

```
regr_sxx (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-30"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-25"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
study_hours (student_id
int,
hours
int,
test_score
int)
as
values
(1,2,55 ),
(2,4,65 ),
(3,6,75 ),
(4,8,85 ),
(5,10,95 ); select
*
from
study_hours;
+ ------------+-------+------------+ |
student_id
|
hours
|
test_score
| + ------------+-------+------------+ |
1
|
2
|
55
| |
2
|
4
|
65
| |
3
|
6
|
75
| |
4
|
8
|
85
| |
5
|
10
|
95
| + ------------+-------+------------+ SELECT
regr_sxx (test_score,
hours)
AS
sxx
FROM
study_hours;

sxx
| + ------+ |

| + ------+

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_sxy"></a>

### `regr_sxy`

Computes the sum of products of paired data points.

```
regr_sxy (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-31"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-26"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
employee_productivity (week
int,
productivity_score
int)
as
values (1,60 ),
(2,65 ),
(3,70 ); select
*
from
employee_productivity;
+ ------+--------------------+ |
week
|
productivity_score
| + ------+--------------------+ |
1
|
60
| |
2
|
65
| |
3
|
70
| + ------+--------------------+ SELECT
regr_sxy (productivity_score,
week)
AS
sum_product_deviations
FROM
employee_productivity;
+ ------------------------+ |
sum_product_deviations
| + ------------------------+ |
10.0
| + ------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--regr_syy"></a>

### `regr_syy`

Computes the sum of squares of the dependent variable.

```
regr_syy (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-32"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: Dependent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_x**: Independent variable expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-27"></a>

#### Example

```
create
table
employee_productivity (week
int,
productivity_score
int)
as
values
(1,60 ),
(2,65 ),
(3,70 ); select
*
from
employee_productivity;
+ ------+--------------------+ |
week
|
productivity_score
| + ------+--------------------+ |
1
|
60
| |
2
|
65
| |
3
|
70
| + ------+--------------------+ SELECT
regr_syy (productivity_score,

AS
sum_squares_y

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--stddev"></a>

### `stddev`

Returns the standard deviation of a set of numbers.

```

stddev
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-33"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-28"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT stddev (column_name) FROM table_name; + ----------------------+ | stddev (column_name) | + ----------------------+ | 12.34 | + ----------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--aliases-6"></a>

#### Aliases

- stddev\_samp

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--stddev_pop"></a>

### `stddev_pop`

Returns the population standard deviation of a set of numbers.

```

stddev_pop
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-34"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-29"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT stddev_pop (column_name) FROM table_name; + --------------------------+ | stddev_pop (column_name) | + --------------------------+ | 10.56 | + --------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--stddev_samp"></a>

### `stddev_samp`

*Alias of [stddev](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--stddev).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approximate-functions"></a>

## Approximate Functions

- [approx\_distinct](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx-distinct)
- [approx\_median](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx-median)
- [approx\_percentile\_cont](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx-percentile-cont)
- [approx\_percentile\_cont\_with\_weight](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx-percentile-cont-with-weight)

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx_distinct"></a>

### `approx_distinct`

Returns the approximate number of distinct input values calculated using the HyperLogLog algorithm.

```

approx_distinct
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-35"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-30"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT approx_distinct (column_name) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------+ | approx_distinct (column_name) | + -----------------------------------+ | 42 | + -----------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx_median"></a>

### `approx_median`

Returns the approximate median (50th percentile) of input values. It is an alias of `approx_percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY x)`.

```

approx_median
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-36"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-31"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT approx_median (column_name) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------+ | approx_median (column_name) | + -----------------------------------+ | 23.5 | + -----------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx_percentile_cont"></a>

### `approx_percentile_cont`

Returns the approximate percentile of input values using the t-digest algorithm.

```
approx_percentile_cont (percentile
[,
centroids ])
WITHIN
GROUP
(ORDER
BY
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-37"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **percentile**: Percentile to compute. Must be a float value between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
- **centroids**: Number of centroids to use in the t-digest algorithm. *Default is 100*. A higher number results in more accurate approximation but requires more memory.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-32"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT approx_percentile_cont (0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + ------------------------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont (0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) | + ------------------------------------------------------------------+ | 65.0 | + ------------------------------------------------------------------+> SELECT approx_percentile_cont (0.75,100) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont (0.75,100) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) | + -----------------------------------------------------------------------+ | 65.0 | + -----------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

An alternate syntax is also supported:

```
> SELECT approx_percentile_cont (column_name,0.75) FROM table_name; + -----------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont (column_name,0.75) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | 65.0 | + -----------------------------------------------+> SELECT approx_percentile_cont (column_name,0.75,100) FROM table_name; + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont (column_name,0.75,100) | + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | 65.0 | + ----------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--approx_percentile_cont_with_weight"></a>

### `approx_percentile_cont_with_weight`

Returns the weighted approximate percentile of input values using the t-digest algorithm.

```
approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (weight,
percentile
[,
centroids ])
WITHIN
GROUP
(ORDER
BY
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--arguments-38"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **weight**: Expression to use as weight. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators.
- **percentile**: Percentile to compute. Must be a float value between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
- **centroids**: Number of centroids to use in the t-digest algorithm. *Default is 100*. A higher number results in more accurate approximation but requires more memory.

<a id="user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions--example-33"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (weight_column,0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (weight_column,0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) | + ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | 78.5 | + ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+> SELECT approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (weight_column,0.90,100) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) FROM table_name; + --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (weight_column,0.90,100) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY column_name) | + --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | 78.5 | + --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

An alternative syntax is also supported:

```
> SELECT approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (column_name,weight_column,0.90) FROM table_name; + --------------------------------------------------+ | approx_percentile_cont_with_weight (column_name,weight_column,0.90) | + --------------------------------------------------+ | 78.5 | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

---

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions"></a>

<!-- source_url: https://datafusion.apache.org/user-guide/sql/window_functions.html -->

<!-- page_index: 27 -->

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--window-functions"></a>

# Window Functions

A *window function* performs a calculation across a set of table rows that are somehow related to the current row.
This is comparable to the type of calculation that can be done with an aggregate function.
However, window functions do not cause rows to become grouped into a single output row like non-window aggregate calls would.
Instead, the rows retain their separate identities. Behind the scenes, the window function is able to access more than just the current row of the query result

Here is an example that shows how to compare each employee’s salary with the average salary in his or her department:

```
SELECT
depname,
empno,
salary,
avg (salary)
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
depname)
FROM
empsalary;
+ -----------+-------+--------+-------------------+ |
depname
|
empno
|
salary
|
avg
| + -----------+-------+--------+-------------------+ |
personnel
|
2
|
3900
|
3700.0
| |
personnel
|
5
|
3500
|
3700.0
| |
develop
|
8
|
6000
|
5020.0
| |
develop
|
10
|
5200
|
5020.0
| |
develop
|
11
|
5200
|
5020.0
| |
develop
|
9
|
4500
|
5020.0
| |
develop
|
7
|
4200
|
5020.0
| |
sales
|
1
|
5000
|
4866.666666666667
| |
sales
|
4
|
4800
|
4866.666666666667
| |
sales
|
3
|
4800
|
4866.666666666667
| + -----------+-------+--------+-------------------+
```

A window function call always contains an OVER clause directly following the window function’s name and argument(s). This is what syntactically distinguishes it from a normal function or non-window aggregate. The OVER clause determines exactly how the rows of the query are split up for processing by the window function. The PARTITION BY clause within OVER divides the rows into groups, or partitions, that share the same values of the PARTITION BY expression(s). For each row, the window function is computed across the rows that fall into the same partition as the current row. The previous example showed how to count the average of a column per partition.

You can also control the order in which rows are processed by window functions using ORDER BY within OVER. (The window ORDER BY does not even have to match the order in which the rows are output.) Here is an example:

```
SELECT
depname,
empno,
salary,
rank ()
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
depname
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC) FROM
empsalary;
+ -----------+-------+--------+--------+ |
depname
|
empno
|
salary
|
rank
| + -----------+-------+--------+--------+ |
personnel
|
2
|
3900
|
1
| |
develop
|
8
|
6000
|
1
| |
develop
|
10
|
5200
|
2
| |
develop
|
11
|
5200
|
2
| |
develop
|
9
|
4500
|
4
| |
develop
|
7
|
4200
|
5
| |
sales
|
1
|
5000
|
1
| |
sales
|
4
|
4800
|
2
| |
personnel
|
5
|
3500
|
2
| |
sales
|
3
|
4800
|
2
| + -----------+-------+--------+--------+
```

There is another important concept associated with window functions: for each row, there is a set of rows within its partition called its window frame. Some window functions act only on the rows of the window frame, rather than of the whole partition. Here is an example of using window frames in queries:

```
SELECT
depname,
empno,
salary,
avg (salary)
OVER (ORDER
BY
salary
ASC
ROWS
BETWEEN
1
PRECEDING
AND
1
FOLLOWING)
AS
avg,
min (salary)
OVER (ORDER
BY
empno
ASC
ROWS
BETWEEN
UNBOUNDED
PRECEDING
AND
CURRENT
ROW)
AS
cum_min FROM
empsalary ORDER
BY
empno
ASC;
+ -----------+-------+--------+--------------------+---------+ |
depname
|
empno
|
salary
|
avg
|
cum_min
| + -----------+-------+--------+--------------------+---------+ |
sales
|
1
|
5000
|
5000.0
|
5000
| |
personnel
|
2
|
3900
|
3866.6666666666665
|
3900
| |
sales
|
3
|
4800
|
4700.0
|
3900
| |
sales
|
4
|
4800
|
4866.666666666667
|
3900
| |
personnel
|
5
|
3500
|
3700.0
|
3500
| |
develop
|
7
|
4200
|
4200.0
|
3500
| |
develop
|
8
|
6000
|
5600.0
|
3500
| |
develop
|
9
|
4500
|
4500.0
|
3500
| |
develop
|
10
|
5200
|
5133.333333333333
|
3500
| |
develop
|
11
|
5200
|
5466.666666666667
|
3500
| + -----------+-------+--------+--------------------+---------+
```

When a query involves multiple window functions, it is possible to write out each one with a separate OVER clause, but this is duplicative and error-prone if the same windowing behavior is wanted for several functions. Instead, each windowing behavior can be named in a WINDOW clause and then referenced in OVER. For example:

```
SELECT
sum (salary)
OVER
w,
avg (salary)
OVER
w FROM
empsalary WINDOW
w
AS
(PARTITION
BY
depname
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC );
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--syntax"></a>

## Syntax

The syntax for the OVER-clause is

```
function ([expr ])
OVER (
[PARTITION
BY
expr [,
… ]]
[ORDER
BY
expr
[
ASC
|
DESC
][,
… ]]
[
frame_clause
]
)
```

where **frame\_clause** is one of:

```
{
RANGE
|
ROWS
|
GROUPS
}
frame_start
{
RANGE
|
ROWS
|
GROUPS
}
BETWEEN
frame_start
AND
frame_end
```

and **frame\_start** and **frame\_end** can be one of

```

UNBOUNDED
 
PRECEDING
offset
 
PRECEDING
CURRENT
 
ROW
offset
 
FOLLOWING
UNBOUNDED
 
FOLLOWING
```

where **offset** is an non-negative integer.

RANGE and GROUPS modes require an ORDER BY clause (with RANGE the ORDER BY must specify exactly one column).

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--filter-clause-for-aggregate-window-functions"></a>

## Filter clause for aggregate window functions

Aggregate window functions support the SQL `FILTER (WHERE ...)` clause to include only rows that satisfy the predicate from the window frame in the aggregation.

```
sum (salary)
FILTER
(WHERE
salary
> 0) OVER (PARTITION BY depname ORDER BY salary ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
```

If no rows in the frame satisfy the filter for a given output row, `COUNT` yields `0` while `SUM`/`AVG`/`MIN`/`MAX` yield `NULL`.

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--aggregate-functions"></a>

## Aggregate functions

All [aggregate functions](#user-guide-sql-aggregate_functions) can be used as window functions.

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--ranking-functions"></a>

## Ranking Functions

- [cume\_dist](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--cume-dist)
- [dense\_rank](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--dense-rank)
- [ntile](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--ntile)
- [percent\_rank](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--percent-rank)
- [rank](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--rank)
- [row\_number](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--row-number)

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--cume_dist"></a>

### `cume_dist`

Relative rank of the current row: (number of rows preceding or peer with the current row) / (total rows).

```

cume_dist
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the cume_dist window function:SELECT
salary,
cume_dist ()
OVER
(ORDER
BY
salary)
AS
cume_dist FROM
employees;
+ --------+-----------+ |
salary
|
cume_dist
| + --------+-----------+ |
30000
|
0.33
| |
50000
|
0.67
| |
70000
|
1.00
| + --------+-----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--dense_rank"></a>

### `dense_rank`

Returns the rank of the current row without gaps. This function ranks rows in a dense manner, meaning consecutive ranks are assigned even for identical values.

```

dense_rank
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-2"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the dense_rank window function:SELECT
department,
salary,
dense_rank ()
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC)
AS
dense_rank FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+------------+ |
department
|
salary
|
dense_rank
| + -------------+--------+------------+ |
Sales
|
70000
|
1
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
2
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
2
| |
Sales
|
30000
|
3
| |
Engineering
|
90000
|
1
| |
Engineering
|
80000
|
2
| + -------------+--------+------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--ntile"></a>

### `ntile`

Integer ranging from 1 to the argument value, dividing the partition as equally as possible

```

ntile
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: An integer describing the number groups the partition should be split into

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-3"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the ntile window function:SELECT
employee_id,
salary,
ntile (4)
OVER
(ORDER
BY
salary
DESC)
AS
quartile FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+----------+ |
employee_id
|
salary
|
quartile
| + -------------+--------+----------+ |
1
|
90000
|
1
| |
2
|
85000
|
1
| |
3
|
80000
|
2
| |
4
|
70000
|
2
| |
5
|
60000
|
3
| |
6
|
50000
|
3
| |
7
|
40000
|
4
| |
8
|
30000
|
4
| + -------------+--------+----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--percent_rank"></a>

### `percent_rank`

Returns the percentage rank of the current row within its partition. The value ranges from 0 to 1 and is computed as `(rank - 1) / (total_rows - 1)`.

```

percent_rank
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-4"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the percent_rank window function:SELECT
employee_id,
salary,
percent_rank ()
OVER
(ORDER
BY
salary)
AS
percent_rank FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+---------------+ |
employee_id
|
salary
|
percent_rank
| + -------------+--------+---------------+ |
1
|
30000
|
0.00
| |
2
|
50000
|
0.50
| |
3
|
70000
|
1.00
| + -------------+--------+---------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--rank"></a>

### `rank`

Returns the rank of the current row within its partition, allowing gaps between ranks. This function provides a ranking similar to `row_number`, but skips ranks for identical values.

```

rank
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-5"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the rank window function:SELECT
department,
salary,
rank ()
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC)
AS
rank FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+------+ |
department
|
salary
|
rank
| + -------------+--------+------+ |
Sales
|
70000
|
1
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
2
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
2
| |
Sales
|
30000
|
4
| |
Engineering
|
90000
|
1
| |
Engineering
|
80000
|
2
| + -------------+--------+------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--row_number"></a>

### `row_number`

Number of the current row within its partition, counting from 1.

```

row_number
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-6"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the row_number window function:SELECT
department,
salary,
row_number ()
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC)
AS
row_num FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+---------+ |
department
|
salary
|
row_num
| + -------------+--------+---------+ |
Sales
|
70000
|
1
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
2
| |
Sales
|
50000
|
3
| |
Sales
|
30000
|
4
| |
Engineering
|
90000
|
1
| |
Engineering
|
80000
|
2
| + -------------+--------+---------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--analytical-functions"></a>

## Analytical Functions

- [first\_value](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--first-value)
- [lag](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--lag)
- [last\_value](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--last-value)
- [lead](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--lead)
- [nth\_value](#user-guide-sql-window_functions--nth-value)

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--first_value"></a>

### `first_value`

Returns value evaluated at the row that is the first row of the window frame.

```

first_value
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments-2"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression to operate on

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-7"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the first_value window function:SELECT
department,
employee_id,
salary,
first_value (salary)
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary
DESC)
AS
top_salary FROM
employees;
+ -------------+-------------+--------+------------+ |
department
|
employee_id
|
salary
|
top_salary
| + -------------+-------------+--------+------------+ |
Sales
|
1
|
70000
|
70000
| |
Sales
|
2
|
50000
|
70000
| |
Sales
|
3
|
30000
|
70000
| |
Engineering
|
4
|
90000
|
90000
| |
Engineering
|
5
|
80000
|
90000
| + -------------+-------------+--------+------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--lag"></a>

### `lag`

Returns value evaluated at the row that is offset rows before the current row within the partition; if there is no such row, instead return default (which must be of the same type as value).

```
lag (expression,
offset,
default)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments-3"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression to operate on
- **offset**: Integer. Specifies how many rows back the value of expression should be retrieved. Defaults to 1.
- **default**: The default value if the offset is not within the partition. Must be of the same type as expression.

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-8"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of the lag window function:SELECT
employee_id,
salary,
lag (salary,
1,
0)
OVER
(ORDER
BY
employee_id)
AS
prev_salary FROM
employees;
+ -------------+--------+-------------+ |
employee_id
|
salary
|
prev_salary
| + -------------+--------+-------------+ |
1
|
30000
|
0
| |
2
|
50000
|
30000
| |
3
|
70000
|
50000
| |
4
|
60000
|
70000
| + -------------+--------+-------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--last_value"></a>

### `last_value`

Returns value evaluated at the row that is the last row of the window frame.

```

last_value
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments-4"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression to operate on

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-9"></a>

#### Example

```
-- SQL example of last_value:SELECT
department,
employee_id,
salary,
last_value (salary)
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary)
AS
running_last_salary FROM
employees;
+ -------------+-------------+--------+---------------------+ |
department
|
employee_id
|
salary
|
running_last_salary
| + -------------+-------------+--------+---------------------+ |
Sales
|
1
|
30000
|
30000
| |
Sales
|
2
|
50000
|
50000
| |
Sales
|
3
|
70000
|
70000
| |
Engineering
|
4
|
40000
|
40000
| |
Engineering
|
5
|
60000
|
60000
| + -------------+-------------+--------+---------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--lead"></a>

### `lead`

Returns value evaluated at the row that is offset rows after the current row within the partition; if there is no such row, instead return default (which must be of the same type as value).

```
lead (expression,
offset,
default)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments-5"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression to operate on
- **offset**: Integer. Specifies how many rows forward the value of expression should be retrieved. Defaults to 1.
- **default**: The default value if the offset is not within the partition. Must be of the same type as expression.

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-10"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Example usage of lead window function:SELECT
employee_id,
department,
salary,
lead (salary,
1,
0)
OVER
(PARTITION
BY
department
ORDER
BY
salary)
AS
next_salary FROM
employees;
+ -------------+-------------+--------+--------------+ |
employee_id
|
department
|
salary
|
next_salary
| + -------------+-------------+--------+--------------+ |
1
|
Sales
|
30000
|
50000
| |
2
|
Sales
|
50000
|
70000
| |
3
|
Sales
|
70000
|
0
| |
4
|
Engineering
|
40000
|
60000
| |
5
|
Engineering
|
60000
|
0
| + -------------+-------------+--------+--------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--nth_value"></a>

### `nth_value`

Returns the value evaluated at the nth row of the window frame (counting from 1). Returns NULL if no such row exists.

```
nth_value (expression,
n)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--arguments-6"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: The column from which to retrieve the nth value.
- **n**: Integer. Specifies the row number (starting from 1) in the window frame.

<a id="user-guide-sql-window_functions--example-11"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Sample employees table:CREATE
TABLE
employees
(id
INT,
salary
INT ); INSERT
INTO
employees
(id,
salary)
VALUES (1,
30000 ),(2,
40000 ),(3,
50000 ),(4,
60000 ),(5,
70000 ); -- Example usage of nth_value:SELECT
nth_value (salary,
2)
OVER
(
ORDER
BY
salary
ROWS
BETWEEN
UNBOUNDED
PRECEDING
AND
CURRENT
ROW)
AS
nth_value FROM
employees;
+ -----------+ |
nth_value
| + -----------+ |
40000
| |
40000
| |
40000
| |
40000
| |
40000
| + -----------+
```

---

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--scalar-functions"></a>

# Scalar Functions

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--math-functions"></a>

## Math Functions

- [abs](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--abs)
- [acos](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--acos)
- [acosh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--acosh)
- [asin](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--asin)
- [asinh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--asinh)

- [atan2](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--atan2)
- [atanh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--atanh)
- [cbrt](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cbrt)

- [cos](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cos)
- [cosh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cosh)
- [cot](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cot)
- [degrees](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--degrees)
- [exp](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--exp)
- [factorial](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--factorial)
- [floor](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--floor)
- [gcd](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--gcd)
- [isnan](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--isnan)
- [iszero](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--iszero)
- [lcm](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lcm)
- [ln](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ln)
- [log](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log)
- [log10](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log10)
- [log2](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log2)
- [nanvl](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nanvl)

- [pow](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--pow)
- [power](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--power)

- [random](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--random)
