- [signum](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--signum)
- [sin](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sin)
- [sinh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sinh)
- [sqrt](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sqrt)
- [tan](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--tan)
- [tanh](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--tanh)
- [trunc](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--trunc)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--abs"></a>

### `abs`

Returns the absolute value of a number.

```

abs
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT abs (- 5 ); + ----------+ | abs (- 5) | + ----------+ | 5 | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--acos"></a>

### `acos`

Returns the arc cosine or inverse cosine of a number.

```

acos
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-2"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-2"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT acos (1 ); + ----------+ | acos (1) | + ----------+ | 0.0 | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--acosh"></a>

### `acosh`

Returns the area hyperbolic cosine or inverse hyperbolic cosine of a number.

```

acosh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-3"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-3"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT acosh (2 ); + ------------+ | acosh (2) | + ------------+ | 1.31696 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--asin"></a>

### `asin`

Returns the arc sine or inverse sine of a number.

```

asin
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-4"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-4"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT asin (0.5 ); + ------------+ | asin (0.5) | + ------------+ | 0.5235988 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--asinh"></a>

### `asinh`

Returns the area hyperbolic sine or inverse hyperbolic sine of a number.

```

asinh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-5"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-5"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT asinh (1 ); + ------------+ | asinh (1) | + ------------+ | 0.8813736 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--atan"></a>

### `atan`

Returns the arc tangent or inverse tangent of a number.

```

atan
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-6"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-6"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT atan (1 ); + -----------+ | atan (1) | + -----------+ | 0.7853982 | + -----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--atan2"></a>

### `atan2`

Returns the arc tangent or inverse tangent of `expression_y / expression_x`.

```
atan2 (expression_y,
expression_x)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-7"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_y**: First numeric expression to operate on.
  Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators.
- **expression\_x**: Second numeric expression to operate on.
  Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-7"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT atan2 (1,1 ); + ------------+ | atan2 (1,1) | + ------------+ | 0.7853982 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--atanh"></a>

### `atanh`

Returns the area hyperbolic tangent or inverse hyperbolic tangent of a number.

```

atanh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-8"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-8"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT atanh (0.5 ); + -------------+ | atanh (0.5) | + -------------+ | 0.5493061 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cbrt"></a>

### `cbrt`

Returns the cube root of a number.

```

cbrt
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-9"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-9"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT cbrt (27 ); + -----------+ | cbrt (27) | + -----------+ | 3.0 | + -----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ceil"></a>

### `ceil`

Returns the nearest integer greater than or equal to a number.

```

ceil
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-10"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-10"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT ceil (3.14 ); + ------------+ | ceil (3.14) | + ------------+ | 4.0 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cos"></a>

### `cos`

Returns the cosine of a number.

```

cos
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-11"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-11"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT cos (0 ); + --------+ | cos (0) | + --------+ | 1.0 | + --------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cosh"></a>

### `cosh`

Returns the hyperbolic cosine of a number.

```

cosh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-12"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-12"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT cosh (1 ); + -----------+ | cosh (1) | + -----------+ | 1.5430806 | + -----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--cot"></a>

### `cot`

Returns the cotangent of a number.

```

cot
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-13"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-13"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT cot (1 ); + ---------+ | cot (1) | + ---------+ | 0.64209 | + ---------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--degrees"></a>

### `degrees`

Converts radians to degrees.

```

degrees
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-14"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-14"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT degrees (pi ()); + ------------+ | degrees (0) | + ------------+ | 180.0 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--exp"></a>

### `exp`

Returns the base-e exponential of a number.

```

exp
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-15"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-15"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT exp (1 ); + ---------+ | exp (1) | + ---------+ | 2.71828 | + ---------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--factorial"></a>

### `factorial`

Factorial. Returns 1 if value is less than 2.

```

factorial
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-16"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-16"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT factorial (5 ); + ---------------+ | factorial (5) | + ---------------+ | 120 | + ---------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--floor"></a>

### `floor`

Returns the nearest integer less than or equal to a number.

```

floor
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-17"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-17"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT floor (3.14 ); + -------------+ | floor (3.14) | + -------------+ | 3.0 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--gcd"></a>

### `gcd`

Returns the greatest common divisor of `expression_x` and `expression_y`. Returns 0 if both inputs are zero.

```
gcd (expression_x,
expression_y)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-18"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_x**: First numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_y**: Second numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-18"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT gcd (48,18 ); + ------------+ | gcd (48,18) | + ------------+ | 6 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--isnan"></a>

### `isnan`

Returns true if a given number is +NaN or -NaN otherwise returns false.

```

isnan
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-19"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-19"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT isnan (1 ); + ----------+ | isnan (1) | + ----------+ | false | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--iszero"></a>

### `iszero`

Returns true if a given number is +0.0 or -0.0 otherwise returns false.

```

iszero
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-20"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-20"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT iszero (0 ); + ------------+ | iszero (0) | + ------------+ | true | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lcm"></a>

### `lcm`

Returns the least common multiple of `expression_x` and `expression_y`. Returns 0 if either input is zero.

```
lcm (expression_x,
expression_y)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-21"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_x**: First numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression\_y**: Second numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-21"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT lcm (4,5 ); + ----------+ | lcm (4,5) | + ----------+ | 20 | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ln"></a>

### `ln`

Returns the natural logarithm of a number.

```

ln
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-22"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-22"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT ln (2.71828 ); + -------------+ | ln (2.71828) | + -------------+ | 1.0 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log"></a>

### `log`

Returns the base-x logarithm of a number. Can either provide a specified base, or if omitted then takes the base-10 of a number.

```
log (base,
numeric_expression) log (numeric_expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-23"></a>

#### Arguments

- **base**: Base numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-23"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT log (10 ); + ---------+ | log (10) | + ---------+ | 1.0 | + ---------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log10"></a>

### `log10`

Returns the base-10 logarithm of a number.

```

log10
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-24"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-24"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT log10 (100 ); + -------------+ | log10 (100) | + -------------+ | 2.0 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--log2"></a>

### `log2`

Returns the base-2 logarithm of a number.

```

log2
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-25"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-25"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT log2 (8 ); + -----------+ | log2 (8) | + -----------+ | 3.0 | + -----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nanvl"></a>

### `nanvl`

Returns the first argument if it’s not *NaN*.
Returns the second argument otherwise.

```
nanvl (expression_x,
expression_y)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-26"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression\_x**: Numeric expression to return if it’s not *NaN*. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators.
- **expression\_y**: Numeric expression to return if the first expression is *NaN*. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-26"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT nanvl (0,5 ); + ------------+ | nanvl (0,5) | + ------------+ | 0 | + ------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--pi"></a>

### `pi`

Returns an approximate value of π.

```

pi
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--pow"></a>

### `pow`

*Alias of [power](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--power).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--power"></a>

### `power`

Returns a base expression raised to the power of an exponent.

```
power (base,
exponent)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-27"></a>

#### Arguments

- **base**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **exponent**: Exponent numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-27"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT power (2,3 ); + -------------+ | power (2,3) | + -------------+ | 8 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases"></a>

#### Aliases

- pow

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--radians"></a>

### `radians`

Converts degrees to radians.

```

radians
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-28"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-28"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT radians (180 ); + ----------------+ | radians (180) | + ----------------+ | 3.14159265359 | + ----------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rand"></a>

### `rand`

*Alias of [random](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--random).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--random"></a>

### `random`

Returns a random float value in the range [0, 1).
The random seed is unique to each row.

```

random
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-29"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT random (); + ------------------+ | random () | + ------------------+ | 0.7389238902938 | + ------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-2"></a>

#### Aliases

- rand

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--round"></a>

### `round`

Rounds a number to the nearest integer.

```
round (numeric_expression [,
decimal_places ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-29"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **decimal\_places**: Optional. The number of decimal places to round to. Defaults to 0.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-30"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT round (3.14159 ); + --------------+ | round (3.14159) | + --------------+ | 3.0 | + --------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--signum"></a>

### `signum`

Returns the sign of a number.
Negative numbers return `-1`.
Zero and positive numbers return `1`.

```

signum
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-30"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-31"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT signum (- 42 ); + -------------+ | signum (- 42) | + -------------+ | - 1 | + -------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sin"></a>

### `sin`

Returns the sine of a number.

```

sin
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-31"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-32"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT sin (0 ); + ----------+ | sin (0) | + ----------+ | 0.0 | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sinh"></a>

### `sinh`

Returns the hyperbolic sine of a number.

```

sinh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-32"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-33"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT sinh (1 ); + -----------+ | sinh (1) | + -----------+ | 1.1752012 | + -----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--sqrt"></a>

### `sqrt`

Returns the square root of a number.

```

sqrt
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-33"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--tan"></a>

### `tan`

Returns the tangent of a number.

```

tan
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-34"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-34"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT tan (pi () / 4 ); + --------------+ | tan (PI () / 4) | + --------------+ | 1.0 | + --------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--tanh"></a>

### `tanh`

Returns the hyperbolic tangent of a number.

```

tanh
(
numeric_expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-35"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-35"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT tanh (20 ); + ----------+ | tanh (20) | + ----------+ | 1.0 | + ----------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--trunc"></a>

### `trunc`

Truncates a number to a whole number or truncated to the specified decimal places.

```
trunc (numeric_expression [,
decimal_places ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-36"></a>

#### Arguments

- **numeric\_expression**: Numeric expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **decimal\_places**: Optional. The number of decimal places to
  truncate to. Defaults to 0 (truncate to a whole number). If
  `decimal_places` is a positive integer, truncates digits to the
  right of the decimal point. If `decimal_places` is a negative
  integer, replaces digits to the left of the decimal point with `0`.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-36"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT trunc (42.738 ); + ----------------+ | trunc (42.738) | + ----------------+ | 42 | + ----------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--conditional-functions"></a>

## Conditional Functions

- [coalesce](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--coalesce)
- [greatest](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--greatest)
- [ifnull](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ifnull)
- [least](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--least)
- [nullif](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nullif)
- [nvl](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nvl)
- [nvl2](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nvl2)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--coalesce"></a>

### `coalesce`

Returns the first of its arguments that is not *null*. Returns *null* if all arguments are *null*. This function is often used to substitute a default value for *null* values.

```
coalesce (expression1 [,
...,
expression_n ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-37"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1, expression\_n**: Expression to use if previous expressions are *null*. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators. Pass as many expression arguments as necessary.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-37"></a>

#### Example

```
> select coalesce (null,null,'datafusion' ); + ----------------------------------------+ | coalesce (NULL,NULL,Utf8 ("datafusion" )) | + ----------------------------------------+ | datafusion | + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--greatest"></a>

### `greatest`

Returns the greatest value in a list of expressions. Returns *null* if all expressions are *null*.

```
greatest (expression1 [,
...,
expression_n ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-38"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1, expression\_n**: Expressions to compare and return the greatest value.. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators. Pass as many expression arguments as necessary.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-38"></a>

#### Example

```
> select greatest (4,7,5 ); + ---------------------------+ | greatest (4,7,5) | + ---------------------------+ | 7 | + ---------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ifnull"></a>

### `ifnull`

*Alias of [nvl](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nvl).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--least"></a>

### `least`

Returns the smallest value in a list of expressions. Returns *null* if all expressions are *null*.

```
least (expression1 [,
...,
expression_n ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-39"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1, expression\_n**: Expressions to compare and return the smallest value. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators. Pass as many expression arguments as necessary.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-39"></a>

#### Example

```
> select least (4,7,5 ); + ---------------------------+ | least (4,7,5) | + ---------------------------+ | 4 | + ---------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nullif"></a>

### `nullif`

Returns *null* if *expression1* equals *expression2*; otherwise it returns *expression1*.
This can be used to perform the inverse operation of [`coalesce`](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--coalesce).

```
nullif (expression1,
expression2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-40"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: Expression to compare and return if equal to expression2. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Expression to compare to expression1. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-40"></a>

#### Example

```
> select nullif ('datafusion','data' ); + -----------------------------------------+ | nullif (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("data" )) | + -----------------------------------------+ | datafusion | + -----------------------------------------+> select nullif ('datafusion','datafusion' ); + -----------------------------------------------+ | nullif (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("datafusion" )) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | | + -----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nvl"></a>

### `nvl`

Returns *expression2* if *expression1* is NULL otherwise it returns *expression1* and *expression2* is not evaluated. This function can be used to substitute a default value for NULL values.

```
nvl (expression1,
expression2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-41"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: Expression to return if not null. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Expression to return if expr1 is null. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-41"></a>

#### Example

```
> select nvl (null,'a' ); + ---------------------+ | nvl (NULL,Utf8 ("a" )) | + ---------------------+ | a | + ---------------------+\> select nvl ('b','a' ); + --------------------------+ | nvl (Utf8 ("b" ),Utf8 ("a" )) | + --------------------------+ | b | + --------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-3"></a>

#### Aliases

- ifnull

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--nvl2"></a>

### `nvl2`

Returns *expression2* if *expression1* is not NULL; otherwise it returns *expression3*.

```
nvl2 (expression1,
expression2,
expression3)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-42"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression1**: Expression to test for null. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression2**: Expression to return if expr1 is not null. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **expression3**: Expression to return if expr1 is null. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-42"></a>

#### Example

```
> select nvl2 (null,'a','b' ); + --------------------------------+ | nvl2 (NULL,Utf8 ("a" ),Utf8 ("b" )) | + --------------------------------+ | b | + --------------------------------+> select nvl2 ('data','a','b' ); + ----------------------------------------+ | nvl2 (Utf8 ("data" ),Utf8 ("a" ),Utf8 ("b" )) | + ----------------------------------------+ | a | + ----------------------------------------+
```

- [lpad](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lpad)

- [reverse](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--reverse)

- [rpad](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rpad)
- [rtrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rtrim)
- [split\_part](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--split-part)
- [starts\_with](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--starts-with)

- [substr\_index](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substr-index)

- [substring\_index](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substring-index)
- [to\_hex](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-hex)
- [translate](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--translate)
- [trim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--trim)
- [upper](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--upper)
- [uuid](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--uuid)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ascii"></a>

### `ascii`

Returns the first Unicode scalar value of a string.

```

ascii
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-43"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-43"></a>

#### Example

```
> select ascii ('abc' ); + --------------------+ | ascii (Utf8 ("abc" )) | + --------------------+ | 97 | + --------------------+> select ascii ('🚀' ); + -------------------+ | ascii (Utf8 ("🚀" )) | + -------------------+ | 128640 | + -------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [chr](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--chr)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--bit_length"></a>

### `bit_length`

Returns the bit length of a string.

```

bit_length
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-44"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-44"></a>

#### Example

```
> select bit_length ('datafusion' ); + --------------------------------+ | bit_length (Utf8 ("datafusion" )) | + --------------------------------+ | 80 | + --------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--length)
- [octet\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--octet-length)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--btrim"></a>

### `btrim`

Trims the specified trim string from the start and end of a string. If no trim string is provided, all spaces are removed from the start and end of the input string.

```
btrim (str [,
trim_str ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-45"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **trim\_str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators. *Default is a space.*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-45"></a>

#### Example

```
> select btrim ('__datafusion____','_' ); + -------------------------------------------+ | btrim (Utf8 ("__datafusion____" ),Utf8 ("_" )) | + -------------------------------------------+ | datafusion | + -------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
trim (BOTH
trim_str
FROM
str)
```

```
trim (trim_str
FROM
str)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-4"></a>

#### Aliases

- trim

**Related functions**:

- [ltrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ltrim)
- [rtrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rtrim)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--char_length"></a>

### `char_length`

*Alias of [character\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--character-length).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--character_length"></a>

### `character_length`

Returns the number of characters in a string.

```

character_length
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-46"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-46"></a>

#### Example

```
> select character_length ('Ångström' ); + ------------------------------------+ | character_length (Utf8 ("Ångström" )) | + ------------------------------------+ | 8 | + ------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-5"></a>

#### Aliases

- length
- char\_length

**Related functions**:

- [bit\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--bit-length)
- [octet\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--octet-length)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--chr"></a>

### `chr`

Returns a string containing the character with the specified Unicode scalar value.

```

chr
(
expression
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-47"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-47"></a>

#### Example

```
> select chr (128640 ); + --------------------+ | chr (Int64 (128640 )) | + --------------------+ | 🚀 | + --------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [ascii](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ascii)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--concat"></a>

### `concat`

Concatenates multiple strings together.

```
concat (str [,
...,
str_n ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-48"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **str\_n**: Subsequent string expressions to concatenate.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-48"></a>

#### Example

```
> select concat ('data','f','us','ion' ); + -------------------------------------------------------+ | concat (Utf8 ("data" ),Utf8 ("f" ),Utf8 ("us" ),Utf8 ("ion" )) | + -------------------------------------------------------+ | datafusion | + -------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [concat\_ws](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--concat-ws)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--concat_ws"></a>

### `concat_ws`

Concatenates multiple strings together with a specified separator.

```
concat_ws (separator,
str [,
...,
str_n ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-49"></a>

#### Arguments

- **separator**: Separator to insert between concatenated strings.
- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **str\_n**: Subsequent string expressions to concatenate.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-49"></a>

#### Example

```
> select concat_ws ('_','data','fusion' ); + --------------------------------------------------+ | concat_ws (Utf8 ("_" ),Utf8 ("data" ),Utf8 ("fusion" )) | + --------------------------------------------------+ | data_fusion | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [concat](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--concat)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--contains"></a>

### `contains`

Return true if search\_str is found within string (case-sensitive).

```
contains (str,
search_str)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-50"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **search\_str**: The string to search for in str.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-50"></a>

#### Example

```
> select contains ('the quick brown fox','row' ); + ---------------------------------------------------+ | contains (Utf8 ("the quick brown fox" ),Utf8 ("row" )) | + ---------------------------------------------------+ | true | + ---------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ends_with"></a>

### `ends_with`

Tests if a string ends with a substring.

```
ends_with (str,
substr)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-51"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **substr**: Substring to test for.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-51"></a>

#### Example

```
> select ends_with ('datafusion','soin' ); + --------------------------------------------+ | ends_with (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("soin" )) | + --------------------------------------------+ | false | + --------------------------------------------+> select ends_with ('datafusion','sion' ); + --------------------------------------------+ | ends_with (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("sion" )) | + --------------------------------------------+ | true | + --------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--find_in_set"></a>

### `find_in_set`

Returns a value in the range of 1 to N if the string str is in the string list strlist consisting of N substrings.

```
find_in_set (str,
strlist)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-52"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to find in strlist.
- **strlist**: A string list is a string composed of substrings separated by , characters.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-52"></a>

#### Example

```
> select find_in_set ('b','a,b,c,d' ); + ----------------------------------------+ | find_in_set (Utf8 ("b" ),Utf8 ("a,b,c,d" )) | + ----------------------------------------+ | 2 | + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--initcap"></a>

### `initcap`

Capitalizes the first character in each word in the input string. Words are delimited by non-alphanumeric characters.

```

initcap
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-53"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-53"></a>

#### Example

```
> select initcap ('apache datafusion' ); + ------------------------------------+ | initcap (Utf8 ("apache datafusion" )) | + ------------------------------------+ | Apache Datafusion | + ------------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [lower](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lower)
- [upper](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--upper)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--instr"></a>

### `instr`

*Alias of [strpos](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--strpos).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--length"></a>

### `length`

*Alias of [character\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--character-length).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--levenshtein"></a>

### `levenshtein`

Returns the [`Levenshtein distance`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levenshtein_distance) between the two given strings.

```
levenshtein (str1,
str2)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-54"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str1**: String expression to compute Levenshtein distance with str2.
- **str2**: String expression to compute Levenshtein distance with str1.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-54"></a>

#### Example

```
> select levenshtein ('kitten','sitting' ); + ---------------------------------------------+ | levenshtein (Utf8 ("kitten" ),Utf8 ("sitting" )) | + ---------------------------------------------+ | 3 | + ---------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lower"></a>

### `lower`

Converts a string to lower-case.

```

lower
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-55"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-55"></a>

#### Example

```
> select lower ('Ångström' ); + -------------------------+ | lower (Utf8 ("Ångström" )) | + -------------------------+ | å ngström | + -------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [initcap](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--initcap)
- [upper](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--upper)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lpad"></a>

### `lpad`

Pads the left side of a string with another string to a specified string length.

```
lpad (str,
n [,
padding_str ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-56"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **n**: String length to pad to. If the input string is longer than this length, it is truncated (on the right).
- **padding\_str**: Optional string expression to pad with. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of string operators. *Default is a space.*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-56"></a>

#### Example

```
> select lpad ('Dolly',10,'hello' ); + ---------------------------------------------+ | lpad (Utf8 ("Dolly" ),Int64 (10 ),Utf8 ("hello" )) | + ---------------------------------------------+ | helloDolly | + ---------------------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [rpad](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rpad)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ltrim"></a>

### `ltrim`

Trims the specified trim string from the beginning of a string. If no trim string is provided, spaces are removed from the start of the input string.

```
ltrim (str [,
trim_str ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-57"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **trim\_str**: String expression to trim from the beginning of the input string. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators. *Default is a space.*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-57"></a>

#### Example

```
> select ltrim (' datafusion ' ); + -------------------------------+ | ltrim (Utf8 (" datafusion " )) | + -------------------------------+ | datafusion | + -------------------------------+> select ltrim ('___datafusion___','_' ); + -------------------------------------------+ | ltrim (Utf8 ("___datafusion___" ),Utf8 ("_" )) | + -------------------------------------------+ | datafusion___ | + -------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax-2"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
trim (LEADING
trim_str
FROM
str)
```

**Related functions**:

- [btrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--btrim)
- [rtrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rtrim)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--octet_length"></a>

### `octet_length`

Returns the length of a string in bytes.

```

octet_length
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-58"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-58"></a>

#### Example

```
> select octet_length ('Ångström' ); + --------------------------------+ | octet_length (Utf8 ("Ångström" )) | + --------------------------------+ | 10 | + --------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [bit\_length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--bit-length)
- [length](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--length)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--overlay"></a>

### `overlay`

Returns the string which is replaced by another string from the specified position and specified count length.

```
overlay (str
PLACING
substr
FROM
pos
[FOR
count ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-59"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **substr**: Substring to replace in str.
- **pos**: The start position to start the replace in str.
- **count**: The count of characters to be replaced from start position of str. If not specified, will use substr length instead.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-59"></a>

#### Example

```
> select overlay ('Txxxxas' placing 'hom' from 2 for 4 ); + --------------------------------------------------------+ | overlay (Utf8 ("Txxxxas" ),Utf8 ("hom" ),Int64 (2 ),Int64 (4 )) | + --------------------------------------------------------+ | Thomas | + --------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--position"></a>

### `position`

*Alias of [strpos](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--strpos).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--repeat"></a>

### `repeat`

Returns a string with an input string repeated a specified number.

```

repeat
(
str
,
 
n
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-60"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **n**: Number of times to repeat the input string.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-60"></a>

#### Example

```
> select repeat ('data',3 ); + -------------------------------+ | repeat (Utf8 ("data" ),Int64 (3 )) | + -------------------------------+ | datadatadata | + -------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--replace"></a>

### `replace`

Replaces all occurrences of a specified substring in a string with a new substring.

```
replace (str,
substr,
replacement)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-61"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **substr**: Substring expression to replace in the input string. Substring expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **replacement**: Replacement substring expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-61"></a>

#### Example

```
> select replace ('ABabbaBA','ab','cd' ); + -------------------------------------------------+ | replace (Utf8 ("ABabbaBA" ),Utf8 ("ab" ),Utf8 ("cd" )) | + -------------------------------------------------+ | ABcdbaBA | + -------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--reverse"></a>

### `reverse`

Reverses the character order of a string.

```

reverse
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-62"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-62"></a>

#### Example

```
> select reverse ('datafusion' ); + -----------------------------+ | reverse (Utf8 ("datafusion" )) | + -----------------------------+ | noisufatad | + -----------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rpad"></a>

### `rpad`

Pads the right side of a string with another string to a specified string length.

```
rpad (str,
n [,
padding_str ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-63"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **n**: String length to pad to. If the input string is longer than this length, it is truncated.
- **padding\_str**: String expression to pad with. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of string operators. *Default is a space.*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-63"></a>

#### Example

```
> select rpad ('datafusion',20,'_-' ); + -----------------------------------------------+ | rpad (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Int64 (20 ),Utf8 ("_-" )) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | datafusion_ - _ - _ - _ - _ -| + -----------------------------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [lpad](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lpad)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--rtrim"></a>

### `rtrim`

Trims the specified trim string from the end of a string. If no trim string is provided, all spaces are removed from the end of the input string.

```
rtrim (str [,
trim_str ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-64"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **trim\_str**: String expression to trim from the end of the input string. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of arithmetic operators. *Default is a space.*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-64"></a>

#### Example

```
> select rtrim (' datafusion ' ); + -------------------------------+ | rtrim (Utf8 (" datafusion " )) | + -------------------------------+ | datafusion | + -------------------------------+> select rtrim ('___datafusion___','_' ); + -------------------------------------------+ | rtrim (Utf8 ("___datafusion___" ),Utf8 ("_" )) | + -------------------------------------------+ | ___datafusion | + -------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax-3"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
trim (TRAILING
trim_str
FROM
str)
```

**Related functions**:

- [btrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--btrim)
- [ltrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--ltrim)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--split_part"></a>

### `split_part`

Splits a string based on a specified delimiter and returns the substring in the specified position.

```
split_part (str,
delimiter,
pos)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-65"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **delimiter**: String or character to split on.
- **pos**: Position of the part to return (counting from 1). Negative values count backward from the end of the string.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-65"></a>

#### Example

```
> select split_part ('1.2.3.4.5','.',3 ); + --------------------------------------------------+ | split_part (Utf8 ("1.2.3.4.5" ),Utf8 ("." ),Int64 (3 )) | + --------------------------------------------------+ | 3 | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--starts_with"></a>

### `starts_with`

Tests if a string starts with a substring.

```
starts_with (str,
substr)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-66"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **substr**: Substring to test for.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-66"></a>

#### Example

```
> select starts_with ('datafusion','data' ); + ----------------------------------------------+ | starts_with (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("data" )) | + ----------------------------------------------+ | true | + ----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--strpos"></a>

### `strpos`

Returns the starting position of a specified substring in a string. Positions begin at 1. If the substring does not exist in the string, the function returns 0.

```

strpos
(
str
,
 
substr
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-67"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **substr**: Substring expression to search for.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-67"></a>

#### Example

```
> select strpos ('datafusion','fus' ); + ----------------------------------------+ | strpos (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Utf8 ("fus" )) | + ----------------------------------------+ | 5 | + ----------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax-4"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
position (substr
in
origstr)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-6"></a>

#### Aliases

- instr
- position

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substr"></a>

### `substr`

Extracts a substring of a specified number of characters from a specific starting position in a string.

```
substr (str,
start_pos [,
length ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-68"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **start\_pos**: Character position to start the substring at. The first character in the string has a position of 1. If the start position is less than 1, it is treated as if it is before the start of the string and the (absolute) number of characters before position 1 is subtracted from `length` (if given). For example, `substr('abc', -3, 6)` returns `'ab'`.
- **length**: Number of characters to extract. If not specified, returns the rest of the string after the start position.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-68"></a>

#### Example

```
> select substr ('datafusion',5,3 ); + ----------------------------------------------+ | substr (Utf8 ("datafusion" ),Int64 (5 ),Int64 (3 )) | + ----------------------------------------------+ | fus | + ----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax-5"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
substring (str
from
start_pos
for
length)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-7"></a>

#### Aliases

- substring

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substr_index"></a>

### `substr_index`

Returns the substring from str before count occurrences of the delimiter delim.
If count is positive, everything to the left of the final delimiter (counting from the left) is returned.
If count is negative, everything to the right of the final delimiter (counting from the right) is returned.

```
substr_index (str,
delim,
count)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-69"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **delim**: The string to find in str to split str.
- **count**: The number of times to search for the delimiter. Can be either a positive or negative number.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-69"></a>

#### Example

```
> select substr_index ('www.apache.org','.',1 ); + ---------------------------------------------------------+ | substr_index (Utf8 ("www.apache.org" ),Utf8 ("." ),Int64 (1 )) | + ---------------------------------------------------------+ | www | + ---------------------------------------------------------+> select substr_index ('www.apache.org','.',- 1 ); + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | substr_index (Utf8 ("www.apache.org" ),Utf8 ("." ),Int64 (- 1 )) | + ----------------------------------------------------------+ | org | + ----------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-8"></a>

#### Aliases

- substring\_index

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substring"></a>

### `substring`

*Alias of [substr](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substr).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substring_index"></a>

### `substring_index`

*Alias of [substr\_index](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--substr-index).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to_hex"></a>

### `to_hex`

Converts an integer to a hexadecimal string.

```

to_hex
(
int
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-70"></a>

#### Arguments

- **int**: Integer expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-70"></a>

#### Example

```
> select to_hex (12345689 ); + -------------------------+ | to_hex (Int64 (12345689 )) | + -------------------------+ | bc6159 | + -------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--translate"></a>

### `translate`

Performs character-wise substitution based on a mapping.

```
translate (str,
from,
to)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-71"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **from**: The characters to be replaced.
- **to**: The characters to replace them with. Each character in **from** that is found in **str** is replaced by the character at the same index in **to**. Any characters in **from** that don’t have a corresponding character in **to** are removed. If a character appears more than once in **from**, the first occurrence determines the mapping.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-71"></a>

#### Example

```
> select translate ('twice','wic','her' ); + --------------------------------------------------+ | translate (Utf8 ("twice" ),Utf8 ("wic" ),Utf8 ("her" )) | + --------------------------------------------------+ | there | + --------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--trim"></a>

### `trim`

*Alias of [btrim](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--btrim).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--upper"></a>

### `upper`

Converts a string to upper-case.

```

upper
(
str
)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-72"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-72"></a>

#### Example

```
> select upper ('dataFusion' ); + ---------------------------+ | upper (Utf8 ("dataFusion" )) | + ---------------------------+ | DATAFUSION | + ---------------------------+
```

**Related functions**:

- [initcap](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--initcap)
- [lower](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--lower)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--uuid"></a>

### `uuid`

Returns [`UUID v4`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universally_unique_identifier#Version_4_%28random%29) string value which is unique per row.

```

uuid
()
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-73"></a>

#### Example

```
> select uuid (); + --------------------------------------+ | uuid () | + --------------------------------------+ | 6 ec17ef8 - 1934 - 41 cc - 8 d59 - d0c8f9eea1f0 | + --------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--binary-string-functions"></a>

## Binary String Functions

- [decode](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--decode)
- [encode](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--encode)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--decode"></a>

### `decode`

Decode binary data from textual representation in string.

```
decode (expression,
format)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-73"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression containing encoded string data
- **format**: Same arguments as [encode](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--encode)

**Related functions**:

- [encode](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--encode)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--encode"></a>

### `encode`

Encode binary data into a textual representation.

```
encode (expression,
format)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-74"></a>

#### Arguments

- **expression**: Expression containing string or binary data
- **format**: Supported formats are: `base64`, `base64pad`, `hex`

**Related functions**:

- [decode](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--decode)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regular-expression-functions"></a>

## Regular Expression Functions

Apache DataFusion uses a [PCRE-like](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Regular_Expressions/Perl-Compatible_Regular_Expressions)
regular expression [syntax](https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/#syntax)
(minus support for several features including look-around and backreferences).
The following regular expression functions are supported:

- [regexp\_count](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp-count)
- [regexp\_instr](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp-instr)
- [regexp\_like](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp-like)
- [regexp\_match](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp-match)
- [regexp\_replace](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp-replace)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp_count"></a>

### `regexp_count`

Returns the number of matches that a [regular expression](https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/#syntax) has in a string.

```
regexp_count (str,
regexp [,
start,
flags ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-75"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **regexp**: Regular expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **start**: - **start**: Optional start position (the first position is 1) to search for the regular expression. Can be a constant, column, or function.
- **flags**: Optional regular expression flags that control the behavior of the regular expression. The following flags are supported:

  - **i**: case-insensitive: letters match both upper and lower case
  - **m**: multi-line mode: ^ and $ match begin/end of line
  - **s**: allow . to match \n
  - **R**: enables CRLF mode: when multi-line mode is enabled, \r\n is used
  - **U**: swap the meaning of x\* and x\*?

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-74"></a>

#### Example

```
> select regexp_count ('abcAbAbc','abc',2,'i' ); + ---------------------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_count (Utf8 ("abcAbAbc" ),Utf8 ("abc" ),Int64 (2 ),Utf8 ("i" )) | + ---------------------------------------------------------------+ | 1 | + ---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp_instr"></a>

### `regexp_instr`

Returns the position in a string where the specified occurrence of a POSIX regular expression is located.

```
regexp_instr (str,
regexp [,
start [,
N [,
flags [,
subexpr ]]]])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-76"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **regexp**: Regular expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **start**: - **start**: Optional start position (the first position is 1) to search for the regular expression. Can be a constant, column, or function. Defaults to 1
- **N**: - **N**: Optional The N-th occurrence of pattern to find. Defaults to 1 (first match). Can be a constant, column, or function.
- **flags**: Optional regular expression flags that control the behavior of the regular expression. The following flags are supported:

  - **i**: case-insensitive: letters match both upper and lower case
  - **m**: multi-line mode: ^ and $ match begin/end of line
  - **s**: allow . to match \n
  - **R**: enables CRLF mode: when multi-line mode is enabled, \r\n is used
  - **U**: swap the meaning of x\* and x\*?
- **subexpr**: Optional Specifies which capture group (subexpression) to return the position for. Defaults to 0, which returns the position of the entire match.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-75"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT regexp_instr ('ABCDEF','C(.)(..)' ); + ---------------------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_instr (Utf8 ("ABCDEF" ),Utf8 ("C(.)(..)" )) | + ---------------------------------------------------------------+ | 3 | + ---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp_like"></a>

### `regexp_like`

Returns true if a [regular expression](https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/#syntax) has at least one match in a string, false otherwise.

```
regexp_like (str,
regexp [,
flags ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-77"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **regexp**: Regular expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **flags**: Optional regular expression flags that control the behavior of the regular expression. The following flags are supported:

  - **i**: case-insensitive: letters match both upper and lower case
  - **m**: multi-line mode: ^ and $ match begin/end of line
  - **s**: allow . to match \n
  - **R**: enables CRLF mode: when multi-line mode is enabled, \r\n is used
  - **U**: swap the meaning of x\* and x\*?

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-76"></a>

#### Example

```
select
regexp_like ('Köln',
'[a-zA-Z]ö[a-zA-Z]{2}' ); + --------------------------------------------------------+ |
regexp_like (Utf8 ("Köln" ),Utf8 ("[a-zA-Z]ö[a-zA-Z]{2}" ))
| + --------------------------------------------------------+ |
true
| + --------------------------------------------------------+ SELECT
regexp_like ('aBc',
'(b|d)',
'i' ); + --------------------------------------------------+ |
regexp_like (Utf8 ("aBc" ),Utf8 ("(b|d)" ),Utf8 ("i" ))
| + --------------------------------------------------+ |
true
| + --------------------------------------------------+
```

Additional examples can be found [here](https://github.com/apache/datafusion/blob/main/datafusion-examples/examples/builtin_functions/regexp.rs)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp_match"></a>

### `regexp_match`

Returns the first [regular expression](https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/#syntax) matches in a string.

```
regexp_match (str,
regexp [,
flags ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-78"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **regexp**: Regular expression to match against.
  Can be a constant, column, or function.
- **flags**: Optional regular expression flags that control the behavior of the regular expression. The following flags are supported:

  - **i**: case-insensitive: letters match both upper and lower case
  - **m**: multi-line mode: ^ and $ match begin/end of line
  - **s**: allow . to match \n
  - **R**: enables CRLF mode: when multi-line mode is enabled, \r\n is used
  - **U**: swap the meaning of x\* and x\*?

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-77"></a>

#### Example

```
> select regexp_match ('Köln','[a-zA-Z]ö[a-zA-Z]{2}' ); + ---------------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_match (Utf8 ("Köln" ),Utf8 ("[a-zA-Z]ö[a-zA-Z]{2}" )) | + ---------------------------------------------------------+ | [Köln] | + ---------------------------------------------------------+ SELECT regexp_match ('aBc','(b|d)','i' ); + ---------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_match (Utf8 ("aBc" ),Utf8 ("(b|d)" ),Utf8 ("i" )) | + ---------------------------------------------------+ | [B] | + ---------------------------------------------------+
```

Additional examples can be found [here](https://github.com/apache/datafusion/blob/main/datafusion-examples/examples/builtin_functions/regexp.rs)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--regexp_replace"></a>

### `regexp_replace`

Replaces substrings in a string that match a [regular expression](https://docs.rs/regex/latest/regex/#syntax).

```
regexp_replace (str,
regexp,
replacement [,
flags ])
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-79"></a>

#### Arguments

- **str**: String expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **regexp**: Regular expression to match against.
  Can be a constant, column, or function.
- **replacement**: Replacement string expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function, and any combination of operators.
- **flags**: Optional regular expression flags that control the behavior of the regular expression. The following flags are supported:
- **g**: (global) Search globally and don’t return after the first match
- **i**: case-insensitive: letters match both upper and lower case
- **m**: multi-line mode: ^ and $ match begin/end of line
- **s**: allow . to match \n
- **R**: enables CRLF mode: when multi-line mode is enabled, \r\n is used
- **U**: swap the meaning of x\* and x\*?

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-78"></a>

#### Example

```
> select regexp_replace ('foobarbaz','b(..)','X\\1Y','g' ); + ------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_replace (Utf8 ("foobarbaz" ),Utf8 ("b(..)" ),Utf8 ("X\1Y" ),Utf8 ("g" )) | + ------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | fooXarYXazY | + ------------------------------------------------------------------------+ SELECT regexp_replace ('aBc','(b|d)','Ab\\1a','i' ); + -------------------------------------------------------------------+ | regexp_replace (Utf8 ("aBc" ),Utf8 ("(b|d)" ),Utf8 ("Ab\1a" ),Utf8 ("i" )) | + -------------------------------------------------------------------+ | aAbBac | + -------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Additional examples can be found [here](https://github.com/apache/datafusion/blob/main/datafusion-examples/examples/builtin_functions/regexp.rs)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--time-and-date-functions"></a>

## Time and Date Functions

- [current\_date](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current-date)
- [current\_time](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current-time)
- [current\_timestamp](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current-timestamp)
- [date\_bin](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date-bin)
- [date\_format](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date-format)
- [date\_part](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date-part)
- [date\_trunc](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date-trunc)
- [datepart](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--datepart)
- [datetrunc](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--datetrunc)
- [from\_unixtime](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--from-unixtime)
- [make\_date](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--make-date)
- [make\_time](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--make-time)
- [now](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--now)
- [to\_char](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-char)
- [to\_date](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-date)
- [to\_local\_time](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-local-time)
- [to\_time](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-time)
- [to\_timestamp](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-timestamp)
- [to\_timestamp\_micros](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-timestamp-micros)
- [to\_timestamp\_millis](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-timestamp-millis)
- [to\_timestamp\_nanos](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-timestamp-nanos)
- [to\_timestamp\_seconds](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-timestamp-seconds)
- [to\_unixtime](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-unixtime)
- [today](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--today)

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current_date"></a>

### `current_date`

Returns the current date in the session time zone.

The `current_date()` return value is determined at query time and will return the same date, no matter when in the query plan the function executes.

```
current_date ()
(optional)
SET
datafusion.execution.time_zone
=
'+00:00';
SELECT
current_date ();
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-79"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT current_date (); + ----------------+ | current_date () | + ----------------+ | 2024 - 12 - 23 | + ----------------+ -- The current date is based on the session time zone (UTC by default)> SET datafusion.execution.time_zone='Asia/Tokyo'; > SELECT current_date (); + ----------------+ | current_date () | + ----------------+ | 2024 - 12 - 24 | + ----------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-9"></a>

#### Aliases

- today

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current_time"></a>

### `current_time`

Returns the current time in the session time zone.

The `current_time()` return value is determined at query time and will return the same time, no matter when in the query plan the function executes.

The session time zone can be set using the statement ‘SET datafusion.execution.time\_zone = desired time zone’. The time zone can be a value like +00:00, ‘Europe/London’ etc.

```
current_time ()
(optional)
SET
datafusion.execution.time_zone
=
'+00:00';
SELECT
current_time ();
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-80"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT current_time (); + --------------------+ | current_time () | + --------------------+ | 06:30:00.123456789 | + --------------------+ -- The current time is based on the session time zone (UTC by default)> SET datafusion.execution.time_zone='Asia/Tokyo'; > SELECT current_time (); + --------------------+ | current_time () | + --------------------+ | 15:30:00.123456789 | + --------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--current_timestamp"></a>

### `current_timestamp`

*Alias of [now](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--now).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date_bin"></a>

### `date_bin`

Calculates time intervals and returns the start of the interval nearest to the specified timestamp. Use `date_bin` to downsample time series data by grouping rows into time-based “bins” or “windows” and applying an aggregate or selector function to each window.

For example, if you “bin” or “window” data into 15 minute intervals, an input timestamp of `2023-01-01T18:18:18Z` will be updated to the start time of the 15 minute bin it is in: `2023-01-01T18:15:00Z`.

```
date_bin (interval,
expression,
origin - timestamp)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-80"></a>

#### Arguments

- **interval**: Bin interval.
- **expression**: Time expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function.
- **origin-timestamp**: Optional. Starting point used to determine bin boundaries. If not specified defaults 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (the UNIX epoch in UTC). The following intervals are supported:

  - nanoseconds
  - microseconds
  - milliseconds
  - seconds
  - minutes
  - hours
  - days
  - weeks
  - months
  - years
  - century

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-81"></a>

#### Example

```
-- Bin the timestamp into 1 day intervals>
SELECT
date_bin (interval
'1 day',
time)
as
bin FROM
VALUES
('2023-01-01T18:18:18Z' ),
('2023-01-03T19:00:03Z')
t (time ); + ---------------------+ |
bin
| + ---------------------+ |
2023 - 01 - 01 T00:00:00
| |
2023 - 01 - 03 T00:00:00
| + ---------------------+ 2
row (s)
fetched.-- Bin the timestamp into 1 day intervals starting at 3AM on  2023-01-01>
SELECT
date_bin (interval
'1 day',
time,
'2023-01-01T03:00:00')
as
bin FROM
VALUES
('2023-01-01T18:18:18Z' ),
('2023-01-03T19:00:03Z')
t (time ); + ---------------------+ |
bin
| + ---------------------+ |
2023 - 01 - 01 T03:00:00
| |
2023 - 01 - 03 T03:00:00
| + ---------------------+ 2
row (s)
fetched.-- Bin the time into 15 minute intervals starting at 1 min>
SELECT
date_bin (interval
'15 minutes',
time,
TIME
'00:01:00')
as
bin FROM
VALUES
(TIME
'02:18:18' ),
(TIME
'19:00:03')
t (time ); + ----------+ |
bin
| + ----------+ |
02:16:00
| |
18:46:00
| + ----------+ 2
row (s)
fetched.
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date_format"></a>

### `date_format`

*Alias of [to\_char](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--to-char).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date_part"></a>

### `date_part`

Returns the specified part of the date as an integer.

```
date_part (part,
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-81"></a>

#### Arguments

- **part**: Part of the date to return. The following date parts are supported:

  - year
  - isoyear (ISO 8601 week-numbering year)
  - quarter (emits value in inclusive range [1, 4] based on which quartile of the year the date is in)
  - month
  - week (week of the year)
  - day (day of the month)
  - hour
  - minute
  - second
  - millisecond
  - microsecond
  - nanosecond
  - dow (day of the week where Sunday is 0)
  - doy (day of the year)
  - epoch (seconds since Unix epoch for timestamps/dates, total seconds for intervals)
  - isodow (ISO 8601 day of the week where Monday is 1 and Sunday is 7)
- **expression**: Time expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-82"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT date_part ('year','2024-05-01T00:00:00' ); + -----------------------------------------------------+ | date_part (Utf8 ("year" ),Utf8 ("2024-05-01T00:00:00" )) | + -----------------------------------------------------+ | 2024 | + -----------------------------------------------------+> SELECT extract (day FROM timestamp '2024-05-01T00:00:00' ); + ----------------------------------------------------+ | date_part (Utf8 ("DAY" ),Utf8 ("2024-05-01T00:00:00" )) | + ----------------------------------------------------+ | 1 | + ----------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--alternative-syntax-6"></a>

#### Alternative Syntax

```
extract (field
FROM
source)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-10"></a>

#### Aliases

- datepart

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date_trunc"></a>

### `date_trunc`

Truncates a timestamp or time value to a specified precision.

```
date_trunc (precision,
expression)
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--arguments-82"></a>

#### Arguments

- **precision**: Time precision to truncate to. The following precisions are supported:

  For Timestamp types:

  - year / YEAR
  - quarter / QUARTER
  - month / MONTH
  - week / WEEK
  - day / DAY
  - hour / HOUR
  - minute / MINUTE
  - second / SECOND
  - millisecond / MILLISECOND
  - microsecond / MICROSECOND

  For Time types (hour, minute, second, millisecond, microsecond only):

  - hour / HOUR
  - minute / MINUTE
  - second / SECOND
  - millisecond / MILLISECOND
  - microsecond / MICROSECOND
- **expression**: Timestamp or time expression to operate on. Can be a constant, column, or function.

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--example-83"></a>

#### Example

```
> SELECT date_trunc ('month','2024-05-15T10:30:00' ); + -----------------------------------------------+ | date_trunc (Utf8 ("month" ),Utf8 ("2024-05-15T10:30:00" )) | + -----------------------------------------------+ | 2024 - 05 - 01 T00:00:00 | + -----------------------------------------------+> SELECT date_trunc ('hour','2024-05-15T10:30:00' ); + ----------------------------------------------+ | date_trunc (Utf8 ("hour" ),Utf8 ("2024-05-15T10:30:00" )) | + ----------------------------------------------+ | 2024 - 05 - 15 T10:00:00 | + ----------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--aliases-11"></a>

#### Aliases

- datetrunc

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--datepart"></a>

### `datepart`

*Alias of [date\_part](#user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--date-part).*

<a id="user-guide-sql-scalar_functions--datetrunc"></a>

### `datetrunc`
